name: Bidirectional Typing
summary: Inference split into two modes: synthesis (infer a type from an expression) and checking (verify an expression against an expected type). Used when full inference is undecidable, expensive, or hostile to diagnostics; lets expected types flow into lambdas, patterns, literals, and holes while obvious expressions still synthesize.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Bidirectional typing splits typing into two modes:

- **synthesis**: infer a type from an expression;
- **checking**: verify an expression against an expected type.

This technique is especially useful when full inference is undecidable, too expensive, or hostile to diagnostics. It lets expected types flow into lambdas, patterns, literals, and holes, while still allowing obvious expressions to synthesize types.

Bidirectional typing has become the default practical pattern for expressive type systems precisely because it balances inference and annotation requirements: programmers annotate at boundaries (function signatures, top-level declarations) and the checker propagates expected types inward.

sections.title: Why bidirectional typing improves diagnostics
sections.content:
Bidirectional typing improves error locality because the checker often knows what type was expected at the exact subexpression that fails. A unification-based HM solver may only discover an error long after the offending expression was traversed; a bidirectional checker fails the moment a subexpression cannot meet the expected shape, with the expected type already in hand to render in the diagnostic.

This is why most modern dependent and effect-typed languages adopt bidirectional discipline as the inference backbone: it keeps error messages local even when the type system is too rich for global inference.

sections.title: Where bidirectional typing scales well
Judgement example
sections.content:
Bidirectional typing is often written with two judgments:

```text
Γ ⊢ expression ⇒ Type      // synthesize: infer type from expression
Γ ⊢ expression ⇐ Type      // check: verify expression against expected type
```

For example, a variable synthesizes from the context, while a lambda usually checks against an expected function type:

```text
Γ(x) = Int
Γ ⊢ x ⇒ Int

Γ, x:A ⊢ body ⇐ B
Γ ⊢ (fn x => body) ⇐ A -> B
```

The diagnostic advantage is immediate: if the body fails to check, the compiler already knows the expected result type `B` and can report the error at the body rather than at a later unification site.
